item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cliché "Note at The End" aside, I enjoyed this! I always find it interesting and can appreciate when the Serpent's Hand is portrayed as knowing more than the Foundation — it makes sense in most canons, given how open and interactive they are with the anomalous, in contrast to the Foundation's staunch bureaucracy.

I wish this were portrayed without the sharp contrast between the Foundation's documentation and the magical tone of the Hand. There's the issue of us, the readers, being given a much more "fantastical" story than what we've just read, with seemingly minimal connective tissue between the two — which is a familiar trend in Hand articles. And I'm not entirely a fan of that; I prefer consistent tone, or tone that can flow neatly between extremes. But that's probably more a matter of personal tastes than a problem with the writing: This succeeds in getting me interested and wanting more.

Otherwise, I'm thinking that they (the Hand) is well-characterized. Despite their knowledge, even they have their limits, but they at least had the integrity to try to ensure that nothing went awry, left a "contingency" of sorts for their successors, and even reached out to the Foundation. It gives them depth. Lots to enjoy here.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image is sourced to here, under Public Domain.

The second image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Our Bellmaker drew upon all the worlds in the expanse of chaos as his inspiration. When his bell tolled, the skies above our town gave way to fantastic places even we could scarcely understand. The bell would toll twelve times a day, each toll would open passages to different places. Even those that did not travel the pathways of the bell could look upon the places and visions in the sky and bask in their majesty.

it make me yearn for
I thoroughly enjoyed this :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this has the most keter name ive seen in a long time. I'm glad it didn't disappoint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, but I'm getting awfully irritated at all of the unknown substances that are completely indestructible.

Hint at composition, or at least give contradictory results… Make it strong, prohibitively strong by all means … but leave some room for physics and chemistry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's fine, but it relies entirely on the letter. Everything else is basically expendable. You could post the letter as a tale and it would work totally well. For these reason, I don't think it works tremendously well as an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bell would toll twelve times a day, each toll would open passages to different places.

On ██/██/20██, during the SCP-1527-1 activation event, SCP-1527-1 tolled 11 times, surpassing the previously recorded total. No SCP-1527-A entities were produced by the event: the reason for this is unclear.

Oh god.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In accordance with the Creative Commons License, I have removed the two images featured in this article because they were originally watermarked (which I had cropped). I have received no complaints about my use of these images and do this under no external influence. I apologize for this incident.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letter sells me hard on this. The tone, the way it references the Foundation, everything. Excellent. I was looking negatively for a while due to the indestructible part, but the letter and the bellmaker description made it feel right. Fantastic. The last addendum is good and ketery (I'm making that a word now), but i just love the letter too much. The image of a Hand operative having to try and set the place up in the hopes that the opposition will be able to contasin it where they failed… It's a powerful story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I said both are acceptable, I never said they were mutually exclusive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why can't it be both? D:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ SPOILER
- I TOLD YOU

There are two ways the last addendum can be read, both of which are acceptable (at least in my headcanon).

1. No Opal Shells were sent, but rather something much worse that the Foundation couldn't even detect.

2. Nothing really was sent: the Bellmaker purposefully sent nothing after making it toll eleven times (which would make the Foundation think something bad was about to happen) just because the confusion over why there was nothing would scare them even more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the Serpent's Hand angle, but that last addendum really is the best part. For a Keter, this works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A great read. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The things keep coming. The flow cannot be stopped, only contained, and that with great effort. And they exhibit adaptive behavior; using the famed "locked box" metaphor, if you locked this SCP away, it would find a way to escape and do - well, whatever it is that opalescent crustaceans do after breaking containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept of the thing's origin, but I don't like how it was blatantly thrown out via the letter.

Also, I think the final addendum kinda went over my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although I think we're all getting bored of the whole "Let's put an addendum right at the end that suggests something even worse is about to happen with this SCP", I still enjoyed this scip. As with others I liked the idea of the Serpent's Hand saying "welp, you're the SCP and you're not just gonna let one of our little fuck-ups destroy the world. Enjoy!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly this. I also believe SCPs are unable to self-termination since they are not working for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this, especially the idea of the Hand using the Foundation to perform tasks they either cannot or will not do. Enthusiastically upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tone is just wobbly.

I was lost until the part where they adapted, then I said okay, whatever. Then I read about a universe I do not care about, and was not made to care about, to replace a hook. This is is the pot that makes monsters, except the Serpent's Hand wrapped it up for the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmm, no, feels too generic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If "fighting hordes of Eldritch horrors every night with no clear idea how to stop them" does not classify as Keter for you, I'd like to see what does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Termination" is only correct as a euphemism for when the Foundation deliberately executes someone. It's possible for Foundation personnel to self-terminate in the line of duty, but a Serpent's Hand operative commits suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
they began to vocalize the following utterances:

That's a horribly awkward way to say "They said something". Fix this please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool shit, nice voice and pictures. Any way we could get an estimate of the size of the community?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Keter status seems unnecessary to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rest assured, this has nothing to do with the vote. It's about unfair criticism. But I agree that further discussion won't lead to anything worthwhile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some elements you missed, namely regarding the "why" he's just sending in rainbow crabs, and whether or not that's going to change. You also really don't need to read other SH articles to understand this, just reading the description on the GoI list should be sufficient. Additionally, I have no issues at all with you downvoting this, and like FaustoV, request that this debate cease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certain suggestions you are making go against the Foundation's core nature. They investigate anomalies, but they don't mess with the most dangerous ones. That's why the Foundation still does the Procedure 110-Montauk instead of launching 231-7 into the sun. By the way, I didn't write it, but I'm just standing my point of view at the same time I will respect yours.

Now, I'd suggest to stop this discussion right now. There's not need for any senior staff to stop us like litle brats figthing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read the GoI information, I don't think that helps this SCP. I'm not going to read a bunch of tales just to understand one SCP. (They are supposed to stand on their own, after all.) There are many members on this site: some new, some old, and I've already learned that some are more familiar with different groups and ideas than others. I read the 001 Factory by Bright before I knew anything about the Factory, and its still a great article. I've read other SCPs that list other groups, some I've upvoted, some I haven't.

Even if I had read all that knowledge, I'm not sure that would help my appreciation of the SCP, unless I'm really missing something. And I'm not sure how to explain it more than I have… its just… generic.

You've got a bell, that rings, and creates crab monsters that people have to shoot to contain. There's nothing creepy about that. Nothing funny. Nothing cool. It's got the cliche of 'needs armed men with guns to shoot monsters' to contain it. There's no cool exploration logs about going to other place, there's no motive behind the crab monsters except that they're evil.

The backstory, again, piques my interest, but not enough to save the rest of the SCP. This guy of extreme power, who went to countless dimensions near and far, some material, some immaterial… and the best you could come up with was that he decides to torment his banishers by sending them crab monsters every night. Where did he go? What else did he see? These are better answers you could explore in an SCP, not have him out for petty crab-monster revenge.

Look, this seems to be appealing to many others, just not me personally. You can't please everyone. If you like your idea, and it continues to do well, then I wouldn't change it if I were you. But if you decide you want to go in a different direction, then personally, based on your backstory, I think you can do a LOT better. One possibly stupid idea: maybe its a two-way portal, and there is a way to control where it opens by how you ring the bell (number of rings, duration of each) and the Foundation tried to do it, it worked a few times, failed a few times, but enough bad things happened that they halted testing. Maybe water started gushing in through a portal at some point. Crab monsters invaded at some point. One time shoggoths. They reached an anti-matter dimension and were able to check it out but then it blew up a portion of the desert. I don't know, just brainstorming.

As you said yourself in the letter, as it stands, its mundane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And when He says it's major GOI, it means it also has its own site page.

E: The hook is not a scary twist, but an emotional one. Anything capable to provoke an emotional response of any kind from the reader counts as "hook". Now, while you may say it didn't provoke on you, that doesn't mean it didn't in another readers (e.g Crayne and me).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me alot of this. In a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All of that is fine, but you still haven't told me what is too simple about this scip.

Just because you don't have the necessary context doesn't make it simple.It just means you're not making enough of an effort to invest in this community. If this article hinged on some obscure piece of knowledge or canon spread across half a dozen articles and a handful of tales, then yes, you'd be right. But this is a major GoI…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I partially agree with what you are saying.

SCP-087 is downright creepy/scary. This is not, it doesn't try to be and that's okay.
SCP-1733 and SCP-1981 are both _completely_ unique, creepy ideas that I would never have been able to come up with myself. They surprised me and interested me. They don't have hooks, they ARE hooks. They ARE engaging. This is not, it is a random video game scenario.

Of the ones you mentioned, really 1048 is only one that I would call an otherwise simple/silly concept with a 'hook,' something that really turns the article around. At first you think its a simple Teddy Bear, and it turns out its this disgusting lovecraftian wannabe creating duplicates out of ears and aborted fetuses. That article is a good read, I enjoy it, its still surprising and interesting and unique to me.

While this has a somewhat interesting back story, I don't find it scary or emotional or unique in any way, and slightly interesting isn't enough to make me enjoy it as a whole. I've reread the whole thing 4-5 times now and I'm still not liking it any more.

Your mileage may vary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, when you think in the core idea of several of the top rated SCPs are actually simple (and sometimes silly), but they always have some kind of punchline that makes it special and deeper and/or darker of what it is. In this place that is called "the hook" (wich is a twist within the article's plot that may or not change the entire way you view an article). That's why SCP-087, SCP-1733 and SCP-1981 are some of the scariest things on the site; SCP-093 one of the greatest articles; the reason of why articles like SCP-1048, SCP-1440 and SCP-1548 are so good despite their weird (and half silly) core idea. The hook is what differences a generic item/monster into a real and scary or emotional article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(believed to have self-terminated)

You might switch that out to "committed suicide." Makes the Hand member more humanized and "termination" has a tendency to be used in a specific context. Just my $0.02. Overall, I liked it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edjumacate thyself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure. I am coming in with very little background knowledge of the lore. I haven't ready m(any) SCP tales or fanfiction. I'm actually fairly new to the site, though I've been lurking for a few months. I know nothing about the "Serpent's Hand" but it seems like there is some history with this organization that I know nothing about.

Having said that, my understanding is that SCPs are supposed to stand well on their own, requiring and influencing little-to-no outside information. I feel like this SCP does _not_ do that, and seems to me like it requires some context, which I do not have.

So when you boil it down, you've tower with a bell that rings that spawns crab monsters. Seems like a random Diablo 2/Generic Video Game level.

Like I said, the letter is interesting. Its a cool backstory… a guy who can create portals and all that. But _that's_ what's cool, the story. not the SCP itself. The SCP itself is quite generic, with no guesswork or mystery or weird creepiness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you explain to me what exactly you find too simple about this? Personally, all I find too simple here is your assessment of this scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the letter, but not the rest of it. Too simple. A portal that spawns crab monsters. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Time to change my headcanon once again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is mechanically…loose. I like your concept, but there's something about your writing style here that rubs me the wrong way. You use short sentences when you don't need to, and I feel this could've used a copy-edit or two.

This could be great, but the awkward grammar kept breaking immersion for me. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral for now. I'm going to come back later and re-read. One little peeve:

The architecture and artifacts of the building appear to be associated with several discreet religious bodies or practices

Did you mean "discrete"? (Although many GoI in the Foundationverse certainly are discreet.)

Edit: Ironically, it seems that I can't grammar this evening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article is based inspired from Majora's Mask and the Ing from Metroid Prime 2, though I can't blame people for pulling Rim parallels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reads a lot like Pacific Rim, if smaller scale. The way that it's getting worse seems artificial, a problem I've noticed with some of your other articles. If you have a sapient agency behind a dangerous SCP, one that was originally human, to have it act in such a mechanical way reads very off. Granted, that's a problem with my SCP-1500 as well, but it's still not good.

Neutral for now, because a lot of this is well done, but it could all be a lot stronger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor suggestion: "The following letter was discovered by the corpse" sounds like the corpse discovered the letter. Maybe "discovered beside the corpse" or "discovered near the corpse" would be better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it isn't the evil crab religion I thought it was when I started reading, it's good enough for me to upvote. Chilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was all set to neutral vote this (since another portal that spawns monsters wasn't desperately grabbing me, although I liked the explanation of the bellmaker). But that final very final addendum just pushed me into upvote territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the last line, and how it was actively addressing the Foundation.
"Yup, we've fucked up, you may deal with it now."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really ought to remove those brackets from the addendum titles to stay in convention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great use of Serpent's hand as a GoI. I really dig the tone of reluctant cooperation in the letter. Well done. :)

+1
